people.name: Justinian I
people.summary: Born Flavius Petrus Sabatius in modern-day Karasengrad around 483, he was adopted and educated by his uncle Justin I and took the name Justinian. He rose to effective control of government well before being formally crowned, and his tireless ambition — earning him the nickname 'the emperor who never sleeps' — drove a spectacular explosion of conquest, law, and culture that Brownworth calls the Byzantine Renaissance. Brownworth considers him his favorite Byzantine emperor, noting that Justinian dreamed larger than other men and sought to change the world rather than merely live in it.

people.name: Justin I
people.summary: A rags-to-riches emperor who came to Constantinople with nothing and rose through the army to command the palace guard under Anastasius I. He secured his election as emperor through a combination of military positioning and liberal bribing of soldiers. Nearly 70 at accession, he lacked practical governing knowledge but was wise enough to surround himself with talented advisors — most crucially his nephew Justinian, whom he educated and ultimately adopted.

people.name: Zeno
people.summary: The emperor whose remarkable reign immediately preceded the period Brownworth covers in this episode. Despite being deeply unpopular and even having to flee his own capital in disguise at one point, Zeno managed to survive and left the throne far stronger than he found it — a stunning achievement that set the stage for Justinian's later glory.

people.name: Anastasius I
people.summary: The emperor under whom Justin I served as commander of the palace guard. After Anastasius died, the succession dispute gave Justin his opening to seize the throne. Brownworth notes that Anastasius, like Justin, tended toward frugality and left the imperial treasury full — a fiscal conservatism that contrasted sharply with Justinian's lavish spending.

people.name: Euphemia
people.role: Empress consort, wife of Justin I
people.summary: Wife of Justin I, born a slave and formerly a concubine before Justin bought and married her. Despite her own humble origins, she fiercely guarded the dignity of her imperial station and was bitterly opposed to Justinian's plan to marry the actress Theodora. Her opposition blocked the marriage until her death in 523, after which Justinian quickly arranged repeal of the law forbidding patricians to marry below their station.

people.name: Theodora
people.role: Byzantine Empress, wife of Justinian I
people.summary: A young actress whom Justinian met through his blue-faction informer Macedonia, Theodora was beautiful, beguiling, and an ardent supporter of the Blues. She and Justinian accepted each other as intellectual equals from the start, and her prodigious energy matched his own. Her most celebrated moment in this episode is the speech she delivered during the Nika Revolt, shaming Justinian's terrified advisors into staying and fighting rather than fleeing — declaring that 'the imperial purple makes the best shroud.'

people.name: Macedonia
people.summary: A star ballet dancer who served as one of Justinian's informers within the Blue faction, providing him with intelligence about potential threats in the city. She introduced Justinian to Theodora, making her an inadvertent but pivotal matchmaker in Byzantine history.

people.name: John of Cappadocia
people.summary: A man of little education and no charm whom Justinian chose precisely because he could not be bribed. He streamlined the tax system to force the rich and powerful to pay as much as the poor, centralized authority, and led a personal crusade against corruption — but his brutal methods and debauched lifestyle as a drunkard and glutton made him the most despised man in the empire. He was dismissed during the Nika Revolt at the mob's demand, then reinstated afterward with curbed powers.

people.name: Tribonian
people.role: Quaestor of the Sacred Palace, legal scholar
people.summary: An extraordinary lawyer whose wit, charm, and encyclopedic knowledge of Roman law made him Justinian's perfect instrument for legal reform. In a mere 14 months he published the new Codex, and over the following five years he produced the Digest and the Institutes, condensing 2,000 treatises and over 3 million verses into 50 books. Despite these achievements Brownworth describes him as completely and utterly corrupt — an unabashed pagan who would repeal or propose laws depending on who had bribed him — making him the second most hated man in the empire.

people.name: Probus
people.role: Nephew of Emperor Anastasius I, proposed rebel emperor
people.summary: A nephew of the late Emperor Anastasius whom the Nika Revolt mob chose as their preferred replacement for Justinian. Probus had no desire to be emperor and fled the city the moment he heard of their plans. The enraged mob burned his house in frustration and turned to find another candidate.

people.name: Hypatius
people.role: Nephew of Emperor Anastasius I, rebel emperor during Nika Revolt
people.summary: A nephew of Anastasius I who was the mob's second choice for emperor during the Nika Revolt. He hid under his bed when they came for him but was dragged out regardless, carried to the Hippodrome on the crowd's shoulders, and crowned with a golden necklace placed in the imperial box. After Belisarius's troops crushed the revolt, a sympathetic Justinian was inclined to pardon the terrified old man, but Theodora insisted he be executed as a focal point for rebellion.

people.name: Mundus
people.summary: One of three generals who happened to be in Constantinople during the Nika Revolt. He commanded a large contingent of Scandinavian mercenaries and played a key role in the military suppression of the revolt alongside Belisarius and Narcissus.

people.name: Narcissus
people.summary: An elderly Armenian eunuch who commanded the imperial bodyguard and was described by Brownworth as deceptively frail-looking. During the Nika Revolt he took charge of blocking the exits of the Hippodrome, ensuring the mob trapped inside had no escape during the military suppression.

people.name: Belisarius
people.summary: Only in his twenties during the Nika Revolt, Belisarius is described by Brownworth as already perhaps the most brilliant tactician in Roman history. He devised the plan to suppress the revolt and led the assault on the Hippodrome alongside Mundus and Narcissus. Brownworth teases his fuller story for the next episode, calling him the empire's most brilliant and tragic general who would fulfill Justinian's dream of epic reconquests.

places.name: Constantinople
places.summary: The Byzantine capital and the central stage for all events in this episode. It is the city Justin I arrived in with nothing and eventually ruled; where Justinian grew up and exercised power; and where the Nika Revolt erupted, burning much of the city — an event Justinian then turned into an opportunity to rebuild on a grander scale.

places.name: Hippodrome of Constantinople
places.summary: The great chariot-racing arena of Constantinople, seating tens of thousands, which served as the civic heart of the city and the forum for popular politics. The chariot-racing factions — Blues and Greens — competed here and shaped political life. The Nika Revolt began after a race here, and the Hippodrome became the site of the mob's mass gathering, the crowning of the rebel Hypatius, and ultimately the massacre of over 30,000 people when Belisarius's forces stormed it.

places.name: Karasengrad (Bederiana), Yugoslavia
places.summary: The region in modern-day Yugoslavia (Serbia) where Justinian was born in 483, described by Brownworth as Karasengrad. It was a province of grinding poverty that his uncle Justin had also fled in search of a better life in Constantinople.

places.name: Hagia Sophia (Saint Sophia)
places.summary: One of the two greatest churches of Constantinople, burned completely to the ground during the Nika Revolt. Brownworth teases the story of its rebuilding by Justinian as one of the highlights of Part 2, calling it 'the world's largest and most beautiful religious building.'

places.name: Church of Saint Irene (St. Irene)
places.summary: One of the two greatest churches of Constantinople, burned completely to the ground alongside Hagia Sophia during the Nika Revolt. Its destruction, along with most of the buildings along the main roads, left much of the capital in smoldering ruins.

places.name: Persia (Sassanid Empire)
places.summary: Rome's ancient eastern enemy, described by Brownworth as always threatening to reignite chronic and inevitable war with the Byzantine Empire. The Persian threat was one of several pressures making the empire's survival look doubtful in the generation before Justinian.

events.name: Election of Justin I as Emperor
events.summary: After Anastasius I died and the usual succession dispute broke out, Justin I used his command of the palace guard — the only real troops in the city — along with liberal bribery (five coins and one pound of silver per soldier) to secure his election as emperor. Brownworth notes some historians believe Justinian's own machinations were partly responsible for his uncle's success.

events.name: Marriage of Justinian and Theodora
events.summary: After years of being blocked by the empress Euphemia, Justinian married Theodora in a magnificent ceremony in 523, the same year Euphemia died. He had to pressure Justin I to repeal the law forbidding patricians from marrying below their station. Brownworth describes the ceremony as hinting of great things to come.

events.name: Coronation of Justinian I
events.summary: When Justin I died of an old war wound on August 1, 527, Justinian was crowned in an extravagant ceremony. The contrast with his predecessors was stark: where Justin had been frugal and uneducated, Justinian was lavish, a subtle theologian, a good jurist, and a bold visionary — the ceremony itself proclaimed the empire stood on the brink of a new and glorious age.

events.name: Publication of the Codex Justinianus
events.summary: In just 14 months, Tribonian produced the new Codex — a complete recodification of Roman law that removed centuries of repetitions and contradictions, became the supreme authority for every court in the empire, and was made compatible with Christianity. Brownworth calls it the basis of most European legal systems today.

events.name: Publication of the Digest and the Institutes
events.summary: In the five years following the Codex, Tribonian published two further landmark works — the Digest and the Institutes — which for the first time incorporated the principal writings of the ancient Roman jurists into the legal system. Together he condensed 2,000 treatises and over 3 million verses into a mere 50 books, a stunning achievement that came at the cost of tolerating his notorious corruption.

events.name: Nika Revolt
events.summary: On January 10, 532, violence between Blues and Greens escalated when Justinian executed five rioters but two survivors — rescued by monks — were sheltered in a monastery. Three days later, 30,000 Blues and Greens united in the Hippodrome chanting 'Nika' (Win) at Justinian. The mob burned churches, palaces, and most of the main streets, demanded the dismissal of Tribonian and John of Cappadocia, and attempted to crown first Probus then Hypatius as emperor. Justinian nearly fled until Theodora's famous speech stiffened his resolve; Belisarius, Mundus, and Narcissus then stormed the Hippodrome, killing over 30,000 people and ending the revolt. Brownworth calls it the turning point of Justinian's reign.

events.name: Burning of Hagia Sophia and Saint Irene
events.summary: During the Nika Revolt, the rioting mob burned Constantinople's two greatest churches — Saint Irene and Saint Sophia (Hagia Sophia) — completely to the ground, along with most of the houses along the main roads. Brownworth presents this destruction as the very catalyst for Justinian's ambitious rebuilding program, suggesting that he characteristically saw disaster as opportunity.

events.name: Execution of Hypatius
events.summary: After the Nika Revolt was crushed, Hypatius was brought before Justinian, who felt genuine sorrow for the unwilling rebel and was prepared to pardon him. Theodora intervened, arguing that Hypatius had served as a focal point for rebellion and could not be allowed to live lest it happen again. Justinian, as always deferring to her judgment, had him executed.

events.name: Escalating Blue-Green Faction Violence
events.summary: As public anger at John of Cappadocia and Tribonian grew, violence between the Blue and Green chariot-racing factions spilled into the streets with increasing frequency. Justinian cracked down on both factions and limited their privileges, but this failed to stop the violence and ultimately contributed to the conditions that triggered the Nika Revolt.
